intro: Stripping TypeScript to JavaScript means removing type annotations, interface declarations, generics, and enum definitions while preserving all runtime logic unchanged. The resulting JavaScript runs anywhere without a build step, which is useful for lightweight scripts, serverless functions with cold-start constraints, or open-source libraries targeting the broadest possible audience.

diffs.from: Requires tsc, esbuild, SWC, or a bundler before code can run.
diffs.to: Runs directly in Node.js, Deno, or the browser. Zero build step.

diffs.from: Compile-time type checking catches bugs before runtime.
diffs.to: All type errors become runtime errors. Defensive checks must be manual.

diffs.from: Type annotations, interfaces, generics, and enums extend JS syntax.
diffs.to: Pure ECMAScript. No annotations; only language features supported by the runtime.

diffs.from: IDE can safely rename across the codebase using the type graph.
diffs.to: Renames rely on text search. Cross-file safety not guaranteed.

diffs.from: Ship .d.ts declaration files so consumers get type safety.
diffs.to: Ship plain .js. Add JSDoc comments for lightweight IDE support.

examples.title: Stripping types from a typed function
examples.description: Remove all annotations, interfaces, and generic parameters — the runtime logic is unchanged.

examples.title: Removing an enum
examples.description: TypeScript enums have no JavaScript equivalent — replace with const objects or plain string literals.

tips.title: Remove annotations systematically
tips.body: Strip type annotations (: Type) from function params, return types, and variable declarations. Then remove interface, type, and enum declarations.

tips.title: Replace enums with const objects
tips.body: TypeScript enums compile to IIFEs. In JS, a plain const object with Object.freeze() gives the same semantics with no overhead.

tips.title: Add JSDoc for IDE hints
tips.body: If consumers need IDE support, add @param and @returns JSDoc comments. VS Code reads them for autocompletion without requiring TypeScript.

tips.title: Add runtime null guards
tips.body: TypeScript's strictNullChecks were catching nulls at compile time. In JS, add explicit if (value == null) guards wherever the TS compiler was protecting you.
